stem: On each independent trial, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Trials continue until the first success occurs. What is the probability that the first success happens on trial number [[k]]?
solution: The first \( [[k]] - 1 \) trials must fail and the [[k]]th must succeed: \( (1 - p)^{[[k]] - 1} p = \left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[k]] - 1}\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses [[k]] failures before the success instead of \( [[k]] - 1 \).
This gives only the probability that the first \( [[k]] - 1 \) trials fail, omitting the final success.
This swaps the roles of success and failure probabilities.
This is the probability of [[k]] consecutive successes, not a first success on trial [[k]].
This is just the single-trial success probability, ignoring the earlier failures.
This includes an extra factor of the success probability.

stem: On each independent trial, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Trials continue until the first success occurs. What is the probability that the first success occurs on or before trial number [[k]] (that is, within the first [[k]] trials)?
solution: By the complement rule, \( P(X \le [[k]]) = 1 - P(\text{first [[k]] trials all fail}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(X > [[k]]) \), the complement of the requested cumulative probability.
This uses \( [[k]] - 1 \) trials in the exponent instead of [[k]].
This treats the probabilities as if they simply add across trials.
This is the pmf \( P(X = [[k]]) \), a single trial's probability, not the cumulative probability.
This raises only the success probability to the [[k]]th power instead of the failure probability.
This is just the single-trial success probability, ignoring the [[k]] trials.

stem: On each independent trial, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). What is the probability that the first [[k]] trials are ALL failures (that is, no success has occurred yet after [[k]] trials)?
solution: Each trial must independently fail: \( P(\text{no success in [[k]] trials}) = (1-p)^{[[k]]} = \left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the complement — the probability that a success HAS occurred within [[k]] trials.
This uses \( [[k]] - 1 \) failures instead of [[k]].
This is the pmf \( P(X = [[k]]) \), the probability the first success is EXACTLY on trial [[k]], not that all [[k]] trials fail.
This raises the success probability to the [[k]]th power instead of the failure probability.
This treats the failure probability as if it simply scales linearly with [[k]].
This is just the single-trial success probability, ignoring that [[k]] trials must all fail.

stem: On each independent trial, the probability of success is \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \). Given that the first [[j]] trials were all failures, what is the probability that at least [[t]] MORE trials are needed before the first success (that is, trials \( [[j]]+1 \) through \( [[j]]+[[t]] \) are also all failures)?
solution: By the memoryless property of the geometric distribution, the number of ADDITIONAL trials needed does not depend on the [[j]] failures already observed: \( P(\text{at least [[t]] more trials} \mid \text{first [[j]] failed}) = (1-p)^{[[t]]} = \left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^{[[t]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This incorrectly uses the total elapsed trials \( [[j]]+[[t]] \) as the exponent, ignoring memorylessness.
This reports the probability of surviving only the ALREADY-elapsed [[j]] trials, not the additional wait.
This is the complement (probability the next success happens within the additional [[t]] trials).
This double-counts by multiplying survival probabilities for [[j]] and [[t]] separately after already conditioning on [[j]].
This treats the situation as if trial position mattered linearly rather than applying the memoryless property.
This raises the success probability instead of the failure probability to the power [[t]].
